content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


July 16, 1992


Roger A. Brown
38 North Washington Street
Post Office Box 475
Sonora, CA 95370


Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-92-342

Dear Mr. Brown:

You have requested advice regarding the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ We regret that
we are unable to answer your question because you have not
provided enough specific information. (Regulation 18329(c)(4).)
However, we are sending you copies of two letters (Bagatelos
Advice Letter, No. I-91-202; Hunlu Advice Letter, No. I-89-137)
which concern similar situations and should be helpful to you. If
you desire a more specific response please provide the name of
your client and a more specific description of your client's
contacts with legislative officials. For example, you stated that
your client will directly contact legislative and administrative
officials to "coordinate their participation in news conferences."
Does this mean your client will be soliciting participation by
such officials, or merely scheduling appearances by officials who
employ your client, are authors, co-authors, or sponsors of the
legislation, or are otherwise actively involved in supporting or
opposing the legislation?


1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Brown, Roger A.
No. I-92-342
Page 2


     If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Wayne P. Imberi
                                            Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asked for advice regarding lobbying disclosure provisions under the Political Reform Act, specifically concerning whether direct contact with legislative and administrative officials to coordinate their participation in news conferences constitutes lobbying activity requiring disclosure.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide specific advice due to insufficient information provided by the requestor, but referenced two prior advice letters (Bagatelos No. 1-91-202 and Huniu No. A-89-397) as potentially helpful guidance and requested clarification on whether the client would be soliciting official participation or merely scheduling appearances by officials already involved with the legislation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination/partial response letter. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections are present in the document. The question and conclusion content were synthesized from the letter body. The FPPC requests additional information before providing formal advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requestor asked for advice regarding lobbying disclosure provisions under the Political Reform Act, specifically concerning whether direct contact with legislative and administrative officials to coordinate their participation in news conferences constitutes lobbying activity requiring disclosure.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declined to provide specific advice due to insufficient information provided by the requestor, but referenced two prior advice letters (Bagatelos No. 1-91-202 and Huniu No. A-89-397) as potentially helpful guidance and requested clarification on whether the client would be soliciting official participation or merely scheduling appearances by officials already involved with the legislation.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to Roger A. Brown's informal request for advice on lobbying disclosure requirements by declining to answer due to lack of specific information, while directing him to related prior advice letters and requesting clarification on the nature of his client's contacts with legislative officials.
